      Sculptor
   =20
 =20

There is something odd about a publicly traded hedge fund management firm. =
The typical hedge fund management firm is private,=C2=A0owned mostly by its=
 founder=C2=A0(who is also the chief investment officer and chief executive=
 officer),=C2=A0and the rough cultural expectation is that people who run b=
ig hedge funds collect most of the fees from those funds and get very rich.=
 In a publicly traded hedge fund management firm, the CEO/CIO does=C2=A0not=
=C2=A0own most of the company; outside shareholders do.
  [1]

But the CEO/CIO of a public hedge fund firm still runs a big hedge fund and=
 wants to be treated, and paid, like a big-time hedge fund manager; the boa=
rd of directors, in setting her pay, will be inclined to compare her to oth=
er=C2=A0managers of large=C2=A0hedge funds.
  [2]
 And so she and her senior management team will receive =E2=80=94=C2=A0not=
=C2=A0all=C2=A0of the fees from the funds they manage, but something uncomf=
ortably close to all of them,
  [3]
 not leaving very much left over for public shareholders.=C2=A0
And so in the extreme case a publicly traded hedge fund firm should look li=
ke this:
=09Its CEO/CIO gets paid a lot of money, and in total its investment profes=
sionals get paid roughly 100% of the firm=E2=80=99s fee income.=09Its marke=
t capitalization =E2=80=94=C2=A0that is, the value of its stock, or the exp=
ected value of future cash flows to the shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0should =
be close to zero, because the CEO/CIO should be able to capture most of the=
 cash flows from the company.

But the company is public! Anyone can buy its stock. And so if you are feel=
ing confident in your own abilities, a good trade might be:
=09Buy the company for roughly $0.=09Fire the CEO and pay yourself her sala=
ry instead.

If you have a public company where most of the cash flows go, not to the sh=
areholders, but to the CEO, then it is cheap to become a shareholder and lu=
crative to become the CEO. But if you buy enough shares you can make yourse=
lf the CEO. There is an arbitrage.
  [4]

We   talked on Monday=C2=A0about the takeover battle for Sculptor Capital M=
anagement Inc. A brief recap:
=09Sculptor is a publicly traded company that manages about $34 billion of =
client assets in hedge funds, real estate private equity funds and collater=
alized loan obligations. Its revenue  consists mostly of management and per=
formance fees charged to those funds, and its expenses consist mostly of sa=
laries and bonuses to the people who manage them.=09Sculptor is in a long-r=
unning fight with its founder and former manager, Daniel Och, over some com=
bination of economic issues and personal pique. Sculptor=E2=80=99s current =
CEO/CIO, Jimmy Levin, is a former prot=C3=A9g=C3=A9 of Och before the two h=
ad a falling out, and Och has spent years grousing and suing about what he =
views as Levin=E2=80=99s excessive pay package (over $145=C2=A0million in 2=
021, and about $49 million in 2022). The fight, and an earlier bribery scan=
dal, =E2=80=9Chave had an adverse impact on our business,=E2=80=9D Sculptor=
  has said, and also performance hasn=E2=80=99t been great;=C2=A0clients ha=
ve taken money out of its hedge funds, revenue has declined, and the stock =
price is a lot lower now than it was in 2021.=09Sculptor decided to sell it=
self, talked to 70 potential buyers, and ultimately reached a deal with Rit=
hm Capital Corp., a publicly traded real estate investment manager, at $11.=
15 per share, or about $639 million. It announced that deal last month.=09O=
n Aug. 12, a group of hedge fund managers led by Boaz Weinstein and  also i=
ncluding Bill Ackman and Marc Lasry put in an offer of $12.25 per share, th=
ough that offer only became public this weekend.=C2=A0=09Rithm=E2=80=99s bo=
ard has, so far, rejected that offer, because it worries that it is too unc=
ertain: The Weinstein group does not have committed financing for the full =
size of its offer, and it plans to get some of the cash by selling Sculptor=
=E2=80=99s CLO business to another buyer, but does not yet have a definitiv=
e agreement with that buyer. So the board would rather stick with a reasona=
bly certain $11.15 than stretch for a risky $12.25.

I   wrote on Monday that these issues seem=C2=A0solvable:=C2=A0Like, Sculpt=
or=E2=80=99s board should talk to the Weinstein group, and the Weinstein gr=
oup (which consists of rich hedge fund mangers with plenty of money) should=
 get it together to commit for the full amount of money required, and ultim=
ately Sculptor should manage a deal (with Weinstein, or with Rithm, or with=
 someone else) at some price well north of $11.15.
The market also thinks this =E2=80=94=C2=A0the stock closed at $11.81 on Mo=
nday =E2=80=94=C2=A0and, certainly, Daniel Och does. Yesterday he  sent som=
e  letters to the board complaining about all of this and asking for more i=
nformation about how the board evaluated the bids it got (and quoting my Mo=
nday column). His basic view is that the board arranged the sale to Rithm, =
and is blowing off Weinstein,=C2=A0not because it wants to maximize shareho=
lder value but because it wants to maximize Jimmy Levin=E2=80=99s pay: Rith=
m would retain Levin (though at like $30 million a year), while Weinstein p=
robably would not, so Levin would probably rather sell to Rithm, and Och su=
spects that the board arranged that for him. (The board vehemently denies t=
hat, and says that Levin=E2=80=99s future employment played no part in its =
decision.) And so Och=E2=80=99s=C2=A0role is to publicly scold the board so=
 that they are shamed into negotiating with Weinstein to sell at a higher p=
rice.
  [5]

But here I want to make a point about the schematic math.=C2=A0The schemati=
c math is that anyone can buy Sculptor for about $650 million,
  [6]
=C2=A0and Sculptor is a business that pays its chief investment officer som=
ething like $100 million per year. If you could buy the company,=C2=A0fire =
him and make yourself the boss of Sculptor, then=C2=A0you=C2=A0could take t=
hat $100 million a year for yourself. That=E2=80=99s like a 15% annual retu=
rn on your investment right there, even assuming that you can=E2=80=99t gro=
w assets or improve performance or save money by firing anyone else. That=
=E2=80=99s pretty good. It is not quite the schematic math that I began wit=

      People are worried about stock buybacks
   =20
 =20

When I was an investment banker, ages ago, part of my job was to pitch stoc=
k buybacks to companies. Part of this pitch involved comparing buybacks to =
dividends, the other way that companies commonly return cash to shareholder=
s. For=C2=A0me, the advantage of buybacks was obvious: A buyback requires h=
iring a bank and paying it a commission,
  [12]
=C2=A0so I could make money from buybacks but not from dividends. The=C2=A0=
pitch=C2=A0did not highlight=C2=A0that.
Instead the pitch to companies focused mostly on the advantages of buybacks=
 to=C2=A0shareholders.
  [13]
 The main one is that, in the US, a buyback is much more=C2=A0tax-efficient=
 than a dividend. With a dividend, every shareholder gets paid cash, and ev=
ery (taxable) shareholder pays taxes on the full amount of cash. (Generally=
 the tax rate is  15% or 20%.) With a buyback, shareholders can choose to g=
et cash (by selling into the buyback) or not (by keeping their shares). If =
they keep their shares, they pay no tax (now): The value of their shares (h=
opefully) goes up due to the buyback, but they have no realized income. If =
they sell, they do pay tax (again generally 15% or 20%), but only on their=
=C2=A0gain, not on the entire amount of cash they receive.
I have never been entirely sure how effective a pitch that is: A lot of sha=
reholders (401k investors, endowments, etc.) are tax-exempt, and a lot of i=
nvestment managers seem to measure their performance mostly on pretax rathe=
r than after-tax returns, so maybe companies don=E2=80=99t (and shouldn=E2=
=80=99t) care about saving their shareholders taxes. On the other hand corp=
orate=C2=A0executives=C2=A0tend to (1) own a lot of stock and (2) be sensit=
ive to taxes, so they like the tax efficiency of buybacks.
Anyway now there is a 1% excise tax on share buybacks in the US, and  the W=
all Street Journal reports:

A new 1% tax on stock buybacks is starting to increase companies=E2=80=99 a=
nticipated tax burdens, to the tune of over $3.5 billion in the first half =
of the year among the largest U.S. public companies.=C2=A0
Businesses in recent weeks have disclosed what they are expecting their tax=
 bills for share repurchases to be for the first half of the year, offering=
 a first glimpse at the levy=E2=80=99s midyear impact on their financials. =
Booking Holdings estimates a tax liability of $47 million for the six month=
s ended June 30, according to a regulatory filing. PayPal anticipates a $24=
 million bill tied to buybacks for that period, while MetLife expects a tax=
 hit of $13 million.=C2=A0
The tax, which went into effect Jan. 1, is set to cost S&P 500 companies $1=
.6 billion in the second quarter, according to preliminary data from S&P Do=
w Jones Indices, a unit of ratings firm S&P Global. ...
Despite the higher tax obligations, companies are largely shrugging off the=
 tax. From April through June, S&P 500 companies are expected to spend arou=
nd $169 billion on stock buybacks, down about 20% from both the first quart=
er of this year and from a year ago, the preliminary S&P data show.

The math there is $169 billion of buybacks, 1% tax, $1.6 billion of taxes.
  [14]
=C2=A0If companies did $169 billion of dividends instead, there=E2=80=99d b=
e =E2=80=A6=C2=A0not $33.8 billion of taxes to pay (20% of $169 billion), b=
ecause some shareholders are not taxable or pay lower rates than 20%, but a=
 lot more than $1.6 billion anyway.
  [15]
=C2=A0Buybacks are more expensive than they used to be, but still a tax bar=
gain compared to dividends.
By the way, this is a thing I did not know about the buyback tax:
The 1% tax=E2=80=94which was a last-minute addition to the climate, health =
and tax law known as the Inflation Reduction Act passed last year=E2=80=94i=
s levied on net buybacks, meaning total shares repurchased minus new shares=
 issued during the year.
Here is  the statutory text; section 4501(b)(3) says that the taxable amoun=
t of buybacks =E2=80=9Cshall be reduced by the fair market value of any sto=
ck issued by the covered corporation during the taxable year, including the=
 fair market value of any stock issued or provided to employees.=E2=80=9D O=
ne  complaint that people sometimes  have about stock buybacks is that they=
 are a way to sort of disguise executive compensation: A company=C2=A0pays =
its executives $100 million worth of stock, then it buys back $100 million =
worth of stock to avoid the dilutive impact of those payments. I have never=
 found this complaint that compelling (it=E2=80=99s not=C2=A0disguised=C2=
=A0executive compensation; this is all disclosed), but some people do, and =
if you do I guess you should be dissatisfied with the buyback tax. It speci=
fically exempts buybacks that are used to offset stock-based compensation.

will too. Or that is the idea!


  [10] Again, it appears that Saba (=E2=80=9CBidder J=E2=80=9D) was in disc=
ussions to buy Sculptor, but eventually Sculptor went with Rithm instead, a=
nd then after the deal was signed =E2=80=9Ca consortium of bidders led by t=
he founder of Bidder J=E2=80=9D (i.e. Weinstein) put in their bid. =E2=80=
=9CFinancing for their bid for Sculptor is expected to come from their pers=
onal money, not their firms=E2=80=99 cash,=E2=80=9D reported the Wall Stree=
t Journal. Though their bid involves selling a portion of Sculptor (its col=
lateralized loan obligation business) to another asset management firm (cal=
led =E2=80=9CBidder H=E2=80=9D), which would buy it as a firm, not with its=
 founder=E2=80=99s cash.


  [11] =E2=80=9CSuperior=E2=80=9D is defined in a fairly normal way: =E2=80=
=9CThe Company may only deem a proposed transaction superior if its Board o=
f Directors reasonably determines that such transaction is more favorable t=
o the Company and/or its shareholders, taking into consideration such facto=
rs as price, form of consideration, certainty of payment, conditions preced=
ent to closing, competitive factors, and other factors which influence whic=
h of the transactions is in the best interests of the Company and/or its sh=
areholders.=E2=80=9D


  [12] Actually ideally it would involve a derivative product like an accel=
erated stock buyback, rather than just paying $0.01 per share of commission=
s, but in any case a buyback involves some economics to a bank or broker.


  [13] Also some advantages to the company. The biggest is probably that bu=
ybacks are more =E2=80=9Cflexible=E2=80=9D in the sense that shareholders g=
et used to dividends and get mad if they are cut, while a buyback program c=
an increase and decrease in size without locking the company in much.


  [14] I mean there=E2=80=99s apparently some imprecise rounding there but =
never mind.


  [15] Of course the $1.6 billion number doesn=E2=80=99t count the taxes on=
 capital gains from sales into the $169 billion of buybacks, but presumably=
 that number is a lot lower than $33.8 billion too.

10px;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong><em> Money S=
tuff will be off for the rest of the week,=C2=A0back on Monday.</em></p>
<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Sculptor</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is something odd about a publicly traded=
 hedge fund management firm. The typical hedge fund management firm is priv=
ate,=C2=A0owned mostly by its founder=C2=A0(who is also the chief investmen=
t officer and chief executive officer),=C2=A0and the rough cultural expecta=
tion is that people who run big hedge funds collect most of the fees from t=
hose funds and get very rich. In a publicly traded hedge fund management fi=
rm, the CEO/CIO does=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>own most of the company; outsid=
e shareholders do.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D=
"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px=
; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; backg=
round-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the CEO/CIO of a public hedge fund firm st=
ill runs a big hedge fund and wants to be treated, and paid, like a big-tim=
e hedge fund manager; the board of directors, in setting her pay, will be i=
nclined to compare her to other=C2=A0managers of large=C2=A0hedge funds.<a =
class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transpar=
ent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a> And so she and her senior management team will receive =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
not=C2=A0<em>all=C2=A0</em>of the fees from the funds they manage, but some=
thing uncomfortably close to all of them,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=
=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inli=
ne-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a> not leaving very much left over for public shareholders.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so in the extreme case a publicly traded h=
edge fund firm should look like this:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Its CEO/CIO gets paid a lot of money,=
 and in total its investment professionals get paid roughly 100% of the fir=
m=E2=80=99s fee income.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Its market=
 capitalization =E2=80=94=C2=A0that is, the value of its stock, or the expe=
cted value of future cash flows to the shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0should b=
e close to zero, because the CEO/CIO should be able to capture most of the =
cash flows from the company.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the company is public! Anyone can buy its =
stock. And so if you are feeling confident in your own abilities, a good tr=
ade might be:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Buy the company for roughly $0.</li>=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Fire the CEO and pay yourself her sala=
ry instead.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you have a public company where most of the=
 cash flows go, not to the shareholders, but to the CEO, then it is cheap t=
o become a shareholder and lucrative to become the CEO. But if you buy enou=
gh shares you can make yourself the CEO. There is an arbitrage.<a class=3D"=
footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorati=
on: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000=
; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !impo=
rtant; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusin=
ess.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL=
2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDgtMjEvZXZlcnlvbmUtd2FudHMtYS1waWVjZS1vZi1zY3VscHRvcj9j=
bXBpZD1CQkQwODIzMjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV=
3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA4MjMmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef=
4b8524a319a23cB8509c166" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" tar=
get=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;">  talked on Monday</a>=C2=A0about the take=
over battle for Sculptor Capital Management Inc. A brief recap:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Sculptor is a publicly traded company=
 that manages about $34 billion of client assets in hedge funds, real estat=
e private equity funds and collateralized loan obligations. Its revenue <a =
href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0c=
HM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL2RhdGEvMTQwMzI1Ni8wMDAx=
NDAzMjU2MjMwMDAwNTEvc2N1LTIwMjIxMjMxLmh0bSNpNDFmODQyNDIyMjY5NDc0NWJhY2M1NzF=
jYmQxZWZiMjVfMzE/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4992fadf" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" =
itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-dec=
oration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0=
00000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> consists mostl=
y</a> of management and performance fees charged to those funds, and its ex=
penses consist mostly of salaries and bonuses to the people who manage them=
.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Sculptor is in a long-running fi=
ght with its founder and former manager, Daniel Och, over some combination =
of economic issues and personal pique. Sculptor=E2=80=99s current CEO/CIO, =
Jimmy Levin, is a former prot=C3=A9g=C3=A9 of Och before the two had a fall=
ing out, and Och has spent years grousing and suing about what he views as =
Levin=E2=80=99s excessive pay package (<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombe=
rgbusiness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0=
FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL2RhdGEvMTQwMzI1Ni8wMDAxNDAzMjU2MjMwMDAwNzMvc2N1LTIwMjMwN=
DI4Lmh0bSNpNTdjZTBkNDViYjRiNDZhMDhjMDRkNTYzOTNiYjIzODNfMTI3/607f07ceef4b852=
4a319a23cBf525b3c4" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">over $145=C2=A0million in 2021</a>, and about=
 $49 million in 2022). The fight, and an earlier bribery scandal, =E2=80=9C=
have had an adverse impact on our business,=E2=80=9D Sculptor <a href=3D"ht=
tps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3c=
uc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL2RhdGEvMTQwMzI1Ni8wMDAxNDAzMjU2Mj=
MwMDAwNTEvc2N1LTIwMjIxMjMxLmh0bSNpNDFmODQyNDIyMjY5NDc0NWJhY2M1NzFjYmQxZWZiM=
jVfMjUw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBbaee9e00" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> has said</a>, and also =
performance hasn=E2=80=99t been great;=C2=A0clients have taken money out of=
 its hedge funds, revenue has declined, and the stock price is a lot lower =
now than it was in 2021.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Sculptor =
decided to sell itself, talked to 70 potential buyers, and ultimately reach=
ed a deal with Rithm Capital Corp., a publicly traded real estate investmen=
t manager, at $11.15 per share, or about $639 million. It announced that de=
al last month.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">On Aug. 12, a group=
 of hedge fund managers led by Boaz Weinstein and <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9=
idXNpbmVzcy9kZWFscy93YWxsLXN0cmVldC1hbGwtc3RhcnMtaW5jbHVkaW5nLXdlaW5zdGVpbi=
1hY2ttYW4tYmlkLWZvci1oZWRnZS1mdW5kLTgzMzcyNmUwP21vZD1ocF9sZWFkX3BvczQ/607f0=
7ceef4b8524a319a23cBd3ec42e9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope=
" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importa=
nt; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-s=
tyle: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> also including</a> Bill Ackman and M=
arc Lasry put in an offer of $12.25 per share, though that offer only becam=
e public this weekend.=C2=A0</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Rithm=
=E2=80=99s board has, so far, rejected that offer, because it worries that =
it is too uncertain: The Weinstein group does not have committed financing =
for the full size of its offer, and it plans to get some of the cash by sel=
ling Sculptor=E2=80=99s CLO business to another buyer, but does not yet hav=
e a definitive agreement with that buyer. So the board would rather stick w=
ith a reasonably certain $11.15 than stretch for a risky $12.25.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusine=
ss.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2=
FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDgtMjEvZXZlcnlvbmUtd2FudHMtYS1waWVjZS1vZi1zY3VscHRvcj9jb=
XBpZD1CQkQwODIzMjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3=
c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA4MjMmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4=
b8524a319a23cC8509c166" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targ=
et=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bo=
rder-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: =
solid; background-color: #ccc;">  wrote on Monday</a> that these issues see=
m=C2=A0<em>solvable:=C2=A0</em>Like, Sculptor=E2=80=99s board should talk t=
o the Weinstein group, and the Weinstein group (which consists of rich hedg=
e fund mangers with plenty of money) should get it together to commit for t=
he full amount of money required, and ultimately Sculptor should manage a d=
eal (with Weinstein, or with Rithm, or with someone else) at some price wel=
l north of $11.15.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The market also thinks this =E2=80=94=C2=A0the=
 stock closed at $11.81 on Monday =E2=80=94=C2=A0and, certainly, Daniel Och=
 does. Yesterday he <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/clic=
k/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE0M=
DMyNTYvMDAwMTE5MzEyNTIzMjE4MDE4L2Q1MDk2NDhkZXg5OTI4Lmh0bQ/607f07ceef4b8524a=
319a23cBc5e3230f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"=
_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-b=
ottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid;=
 background-color: #ccc;"> sent</a> some <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=
bergbusiness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZl=
cy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE0MDMyNTYvMDAwMTE5MzEyNTIzMjE4MDE4L2Q1MDk2NDhkZXg5OTI5Lmh=
0bQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB29ede13c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"=
itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none=
 !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border=
-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> letters</a> to the board co=
mplaining about all of this and asking for more information about how the b=
oard evaluated the bids it got (and quoting my Monday column). His basic vi=
ew is that the board arranged the sale to Rithm, and is blowing off Weinste=
in,=C2=A0not because it wants to maximize shareholder value but because it =
wants to maximize Jimmy Levin=E2=80=99s pay: Rithm would retain Levin (thou=
gh at like $30 million a year), while Weinstein probably would not, so Levi=
n would probably rather sell to Rithm, and Och suspects that the board arra=
nged that for him. (The board vehemently denies that, and says that Levin=
=E2=80=99s future employment played no part in its decision.) And so Och=E2=
=80=99s=C2=A0role is to publicly scold the board so that they are shamed in=
to negotiating with Weinstein to sell at a higher price.<a class=3D"footnot=
e-index" href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; =
display: inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But here I want to make a point about the sche=
matic math.=C2=A0The schematic math is that anyone can buy Sculptor for abo=
ut $650 million,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-6" style=3D"c=
olor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; =
border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; backgro=
und-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[6]</span>
</a>=C2=A0and Sculptor is a business that pays its chief investment officer=
 something like $100 million per year. If you could buy the company,=C2=A0f=
ire him and make yourself the boss of Sculptor, then=C2=A0<em>you=C2=A0</em=
>could take that $100 million a year for yourself. That=E2=80=99s like a 15=
% annual return on your investment right there, even assuming that you can=
=E2=80=99t grow assets or improve performance or save money by firing anyon=
e else. That=E2=80=99s pretty good. It is not quite the schematic math that=
 I began with =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cbuy all the shares for $0, then make =
yourself CEO and collect all the fees=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it is rel=
ated.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Now, there is a flaw in that math. The flaw is=
 that Sculptor=E2=80=99s future fee income depends on its clients continuin=
g to be clients; much of Sculptor=E2=80=99s money is redeemable without too=
 much notice, so if all the clients are dissatisfied with the new CEO then =
they will leave and the new CEO won=E2=80=99t get any money. If=C2=A0<em>I=
=C2=A0</em>somehow borrowed $650 million to buy Sculptor and made myself CE=
O, presumably all the clients would leave immediately and my investment wou=
ld be worth $0. And for a lot of plausible buyers of Sculptor =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0certainly for Rithm, which is not really in the hedge-fund business =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0the only way to buy Sculptor and get value out of it is by reta=
ining Levin and his senior management team to keep clients happy and keep m=
aking money.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But that is the genius of the Weinstein plan:=
=C2=A0Boaz Weinstein is a hedge fund manager! But also Bill Ackman is a hed=
ge fund manager, and Marc Lasry, and there might be others involved.<a clas=
s=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-7" style=3D"color: #000000; text-dec=
oration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0=
00000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent =
!important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[7]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Surely=C2=A0<em>between=C2=A0</em>them (1) they could run a hedge=
 fund and (2) they could mollify Sculptor=E2=80=99s clients. =E2=80=9CI lik=
e Jimmy Levin, but I also like Bill Ackman, so it=E2=80=99s fine,=E2=80=9D =
some clients might say, and others might not love Ackman but are pretty fon=
d of Weinstein, etc.; with enough names on the list surely most clients wil=
l find=C2=A0<em>someone=C2=A0</em>they like.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Or not, I mean, nothing is guaranteed, and the=
 takeover sure would be=C2=A0<em>weird</em>. Weinstein=E2=80=99s firm, Saba=
 Capital Management, was one of the 70 bidders that Sculptor talked to befo=
re signing a deal with Rithm (it is referred to as =E2=80=9CBidder J=E2=80=
=9D in the proxy statement<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-8" =
style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wi=
dth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !importan=
t; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[8]</span>
</a>), and Sculptor preferred Rithm in part for financing certainty but als=
o in part for client-retention certainty. Any buyer of Sculptor would want =
the deal to be conditional on some number of clients giving their consent t=
o the deal: If all the clients flee immediately, then Sculptor isn=E2=80=99=
t worth buying. Rithm has an 85% client consent condition in its deal, and =
Saba initially asked for 75%. That sounds like Saba=E2=80=99s offer would b=
e more certain than Rithm=E2=80=99s (with a lower client consent threshold)=
, but Rithm would retain Levin and Saba more or less wouldn=E2=80=99t, and =
the board got nervous. From <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.=
com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kY=
XRhLzE0MDMyNTYvMDAwMTE0MDM2MTIzMDQwNTk1L255MjAwMDk5Mzl4MV9wcmVtMTRhLmh0bSN0=
Qk0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8b66d4ec" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"=
itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none=
 !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border=
-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> the proxy</a> for the Rithm=
 deal<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-9" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: =
transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[9]</span>
</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It was discussed that the transaction proposed=
 by Bidder J may make client consent more difficult to obtain because Bidde=
r J had indicated its intent to not have Mr.=C2=A0Levin (considered a =E2=
=80=9Ckey man=E2=80=9D under certain client arrangements) continue in a lon=
g-term role at the Company following the closing of the potential transacti=
on, and instead Bidder J intended that the Company=E2=80=99s investment fun=
ction would be overseen by an =E2=80=9COffice of the CIO=E2=80=9D which wou=
ld include (without limitation) representatives of Bidder J and certain thi=
rd parties that had no affiliation with the Company but would be selected a=
nd hired by Bidder J. As such it was discussed that the client consent cond=
ition as currently proposed by Bidder J =E2=80=94 while similar to the one =
negotiated with Rithm =E2=80=94 may be more difficult for the Company to sa=
tisfy. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Company=E2=80=99s management conveyed its =
view that, given Bidder J=E2=80=99s stated post-closing business plans, ...=
=C2=A0the Company=E2=80=99s management remained concerned about its ability=
 to retain current key employees, and the likelihood of achieving the clien=
t consent condition.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Och, in <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9l=
ZGdhci9kYXRhLzE0MDMyNTYvMDAwMTE5MzEyNTIzMjE4MDE4L2Q1MDk2NDhkZXg5OTI5Lmh0bQ/=
607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC29ede13c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"item=
scope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !im=
portant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bot=
tom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> his letter yesterday</a>, scoff=
s at this excuse:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Special Committee summarily co=
ncluded that the bidders who did not wish to retain Mr.=C2=A0Levin would be=
 unable to obtain client consents to the transaction. But there is no indic=
ation that the Special Committee took any steps to test this hypothesis or =
to reduce the client-consent contingency necessary for the transaction (and=
 thereby put any such consent risk on the bidder). Instead, they used this =
=E2=80=9Ckey man=E2=80=9D issue as an excuse to defer to Mr. Levin=E2=80=99=
s interests.=C2=A0</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">He also complains that Rithm reduced its price=
 because of the expense of paying Sculptor=E2=80=99s managers:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Special Committee=E2=80=99s de=
fense of management=E2=80=99s interests came directly at the expense of the=
 stockholders. Rithm itself initially agreed to offer $12 a share to the Cl=
ass A stockholders, but revised its offer down a full dollar, because =E2=
=80=9Cit would need to spend more money than anticipated in the form of a l=
ong-term incentive plan and retention plan for the Company=E2=80=99s senior=
 leadership.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s all pretty=C2=A0odd. For one thin=
g, if Weinstein and Ackman and Lasry think =E2=80=9Cwe would do a better jo=
b of running this $30 billion of assets than Jimmy Levin does, and clients =
would like us more,=E2=80=9D then it=E2=80=99s not clear that their=C2=A0<e=
m>best=C2=A0</em>move is to pay $650 million to buy Sculptor. Arguably thei=
r best move is just to call up the clients and say =E2=80=9Chey, if you pre=
fer me to Jimmy Levin, why don=E2=80=99t you take your money out of Sculpto=
r and put it in my fund instead?=E2=80=9D If your thesis is =E2=80=9CSculpt=
or=E2=80=99s clients are so sick of Sculptor that they=E2=80=99d love to ha=
ve their money managed by Boaz Weinstein instead,=E2=80=9D you can just off=
er the=C2=A0<em>clients=C2=A0</em>that deal without=C2=A0<em>buying Sculpto=
r</em>. I suppose it is hard to get $30 billion of client assets all at onc=
e, and paying $650 million for them is arguably a bargain;=C2=A0also some o=
f Sculptor=E2=80=99s businesses would continue to be run by its professiona=
ls rather than the Weinstein group. (Also, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.blo=
ombergbusiness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0aW1lcy5jb20vMj=
AyMi8wMy8zMC9idXNpbmVzcy9kZWFsYm9vay9hY2ttYW4tc2hhcmVob2xkZXItYWN0aXZpc20ua=
HRtbA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBb42d8cf9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Ackman</a> and <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9maW5hbmNlL2ludmVzdGluZy9ibGFja3JvY2stY2xhc2hlcy13aXRoLWhl=
ZGdlLWZ1bmQtZ2lhbnQtb3Zlci1jb250cm9sLW9mLWZ1bmRzLTE1YzAwYTI/607f07ceef4b852=
4a319a23cB38ef6161" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;"> Weinstein</a> have done a fair amount of hed=
ge-fund activism in their time, and if you even partly believe my schematic=
 math then this is a very tempting=C2=A0<em>activist=C2=A0</em>situation: =
=E2=80=9CThis company is undervalued because management is too entrenched, =
so if we buy it and fire management there=E2=80=99s money to be made.)</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also it is very fun that the Weinstein group w=
ould buy Sculptor in their <em>personal=C2=A0</em>accounts? Weinstein, Ackm=
an and Lasry=C2=A0all run hedge fund management firms themselves (Saba Capi=
tal Management, Pershing Square Capital Management, Avenue Capital Group), =
but those firms are not, separately or together, offering to buy Sculptor.<=
a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-10" style=3D"color: #000000; t=
ext-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-co=
lor: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: trans=
parent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[10]</span>
</a> Instead, they are offering to buy Sculptor in their personal accounts.=
 Like, their day jobs are managing hedge funds, and then as a hobby they wi=
ll form a sort of hedge-fund-manager supergroup and manage another hedge fu=
nd for fun. There are regulatory complications with a hedge-fund firm buyin=
g another hedge-fund firm; buying it in your personal account is easier, th=
ough I suppose there are conflict questions there too. (If you see a good i=
nvestment idea in your role at the Sculptor =E2=80=9COffice of CIO,=E2=80=
=9D can you bring it to your day job?)</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Really, though, wouldn=E2=80=99t you <em>want<=
/em>=C2=A0to do this? If you are a confident successful hedge fund manager,=
 and you had the chance to get together with your hedge fund manager friend=
s and=C2=A0<em>buy=C2=A0</em>a rival hedge fund manager=E2=80=99s firm out =
from under him, for fairly cheap, wouldn=E2=80=99t you do it? It seems to m=
e to make economic sense as a trade, but also how much fun is that? =E2=80=
=9CWe are willing to bet $650 million that we can do a better job for your =
clients than you can, and that=E2=80=99s the price tag, so bye!=E2=80=9D</p=
>

ink.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLm=
dvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzExNjMzMDIvMDAwMTEwNDY1OTIzMDkyOTExL3RtMjMyM=

Link" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; te=
xt-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-col=
or: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> here</a>=
 saying that =E2=80=9Cthe Basic Labor Agreement (=E2=80=98BLA=E2=80=99) wit=
h the United Steelworkers labor union (=E2=80=98USW=E2=80=99) does not prov=
ide a right to veto any transaction that may result from this review.=E2=80=
=9D (The BLA is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32=

bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid=
; background-color: #ccc;"> here</a>.) It seems to me that US Steel is clea=
rly right =E2=80=94=C2=A0there=E2=80=99s no veto right =E2=80=94=C2=A0but t=
he union does have some power. Specifically:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Any buyer of the whole company has to=
 recognize the USW union and assume US Steel=E2=80=99s agreements with it, =
including the labor agreement that US Steel signed last year. (But the buye=
r can do this unilaterally; the USW can=E2=80=99t say no to the assumption.=
)</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The union has a =E2=80=9Cright t=
o bid=E2=80=9D: If anyone makes an offer to buy US Steel, the company has t=
o tell the USW and give it 45 days to match the offer. But after that, =E2=
=80=9Cthe Company shall not be under any obligation to accept such offer=E2=
=80=9D from the USW: It just can=E2=80=99t sell to anyone else =E2=80=9Cunl=
ess that transaction is superior to the USW offer.=E2=80=9D But if the USW =
offers $32 and someone else offers $35, US Steel can take the higher bid.<a=
 class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-11" style=3D"color: #000000; te=
xt-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-col=
or: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transp=
arent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[11]</span>
</a>
</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The union can=C2=A0assign<em>=C2=
=A0</em>that right to bid to someone else, which is what it did with Clevel=
and-Cliffs: Now Cleveland-Cliffs effectively owns the union=E2=80=99s right=
 to bid.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So this does not actually make Cleveland-Cliff=
s =E2=80=9Cthe only realistic buyer=E2=80=9D: If Cleveland-Cliffs offers $3=
5 and Esmark offers $40, then Esmark wins. That said, the assignment does g=
ive Cleveland-Cliffs some big advantages. For one thing, if you are buying =
a big unionized company, it is probably better to have the union on your si=
de; anyone else is going to be a bit more nervous about labor relations tha=
n Cleveland-Cliffs is, which might be reflected in price.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also Cleveland-Cliffs just has the ability to=
=C2=A0<em>annoy=C2=A0</em>US Steel about the process, which is worth someth=
ing. Yesterday it <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=
32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzc2NDA=
2NS8wMDAwNzY0MDY1MjMwMDAxNDYvYTIwMjMwODIyOC1rNDI1Lmh0bQ/607f07ceef4b8524a31=

lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;"> sent US Steel a letter</a> complaining that (1) it=
 has the right to be notified of any bids for the company, (2) there sure s=
eem to be bids=C2=A0and (3) it hasn=E2=80=99t been notified yet:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Right to Bid provision requires USS to pro=
mptly notify Cliffs =E2=80=94=C2=A0as the assignee =E2=80=94=C2=A0of any pr=
oposal received for =E2=80=9Cany possible transaction=E2=80=9D to sell a co=
ntrolling interest in the Company. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">USS has publicly indicated on multiple occasio=
ns, including in two press releases on August 13, 2023, that it had indeed =
received =E2=80=9Cmultiple unsolicited proposals that ranged from the acqui=
sition of certain production assets to consideration for the whole Company.=
=E2=80=9D It is interesting to note that, today, in your letter to the Comp=
any=E2=80=99s employees, =E2=80=A6=C2=A0you referred to these other proposa=
ls as =E2=80=9Cinquiries=E2=80=9D for the first time. Regardless, to date, =
neither the USW nor Cliffs has received any notice of such proposals or =E2=
=80=9Cinquiries.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">USS must immediately either (a) provide Cliffs=
 and the USW with the required notice of all proposals that have already be=
en received; or (b) if USS has not in fact received any proposals, correct =
its prior public statements.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Not a veto right, not an exclusive bidding rig=
ht, but you can see how that might deter other bidders.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also by the way here is <a href=3D"https://lin=
k.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYnVzaW5l=
c3N3aXJlLmNvbS9uZXdzL2hvbWUvMjAyMzA4MTQwMTk3MjgvZW4vRXNtYXJrLUluYy4tQW5ub3V=

4b8524a319a23cB3b6e860f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" tar=
get=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Esmark=E2=80=99s announcement of its bid<=
/a>=C2=A0last week, which is kind of wild? Esmark Inc. is a private company=

<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Esmark, Inc. announced today a vol=
untary public cash and exchange offer for all issued and outstanding shares=

 USD per share. The initial offer period runs from August 14, 2023, to Nove=
mber 30, 2023, and may be extended. Completion of the Offer is expected in =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That=C2=A0<em>sounds=C2=A0</em>like Esmark was=

re is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32488619.288=

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">After amassing his own fortune through acquiri=
ng companies in the steel business and other industries, the 62-year-old in=
vestor has made a bold offer to buy United States Steel for about $7.8 bill=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are trying to figure out if a potential=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway if I were US Steel I=E2=80=99d be tryin=

<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">People are worried about stock buybacks</h2>
</td>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When I was an investment banker, ages ago, par=
t of my job was to pitch stock buybacks to companies. Part of this pitch in=
volved comparing buybacks to dividends, the other way that companies common=
ly return cash to shareholders. For=C2=A0<em>me</em>, the advantage of buyb=
acks was obvious: A buyback requires hiring a bank and paying it a commissi=
on,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-12" style=3D"color: #00000=
0; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-botto=
m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: t=
ransparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[12]</span>
</a>=C2=A0so I could make money from buybacks but not from dividends. The=
=C2=A0<em>pitch=C2=A0</em>did not highlight=C2=A0that.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Instead the pitch to companies focused mostly =
on the advantages of buybacks to=C2=A0<em>shareholders</em>.<a class=3D"foo=
tnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-13" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration=
: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; =
border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !import=
ant; display: inline-block;">
<span>[13]</span>
</a> The main one is that, in the US, a buyback is much more=C2=A0<em>tax-e=
fficient </em>than a dividend. With a dividend, every shareholder gets paid=
 cash, and every (taxable) shareholder pays taxes on the full amount of cas=
h. (Generally the tax rate is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusines=
s.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZm9vbC5jb20vaW52ZXN0aW5nL3N0b2N=
rLW1hcmtldC90eXBlcy1vZi1zdG9ja3MvZGl2aWRlbmQtc3RvY2tzL2hvdy1kaXZpZGVuZHMtdG=
F4ZWQv/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5bafadbe" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> 15% or 20%</a>.) With a=
 buyback, shareholders can choose to get cash (by selling into the buyback)=
 or not (by keeping their shares). If they keep their shares, they pay no t=
ax (now): The value of their shares (hopefully) goes up due to the buyback,=
 but they have no realized income. If they sell, they do pay tax (again gen=
erally 15% or 20%), but only on their=C2=A0<em>gain</em>, not on the entire=
 amount of cash they receive.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I have never been entirely sure how effective =
a pitch that is: A lot of shareholders (401k investors, endowments, etc.) a=
re tax-exempt, and a lot of investment managers seem to measure their perfo=
rmance mostly on pretax rather than after-tax returns, so maybe companies d=
on=E2=80=99t (and shouldn=E2=80=99t) care about saving their shareholders t=
axes. On the other hand corporate=C2=A0<em>executives=C2=A0</em>tend to (1)=
 own a lot of stock and (2) be sensitive to taxes, so <em>they</em> like th=
e tax efficiency of buybacks.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway now there is a 1% excise tax on share b=
uybacks in the US, and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/c=
lick/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9uZXctdS1zLWJ1eW=
JhY2stdGF4LWhpdHMtY29tcGFuaWVzLXdpdGgtMy01LWJpbGxpb24tYnVyZGVuLTQ1MmMxZjkzP=
21vZD1pdHBfd3NqJnJ1PXlhaG9v/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe7794371" itemprop=3D"=
StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #00000=
0; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-botto=
m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> the=
 Wall Street Journal reports</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A new 1% tax on stock buybacks is starting to =
increase companies=E2=80=99 anticipated tax burdens, to the tune of over $3=
.5 billion in the first half of the year among the largest U.S. public comp=
anies.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Businesses in recent weeks have disclosed what=
 they are expecting their tax bills for share repurchases to be for the fir=
st half of the year, offering a first glimpse at the levy=E2=80=99s midyear=
 impact on their financials. Booking Holdings estimates a tax liability of =
$47 million for the six months ended June 30, according to a regulatory fil=
ing. PayPal anticipates a $24 million bill tied to buybacks for that period=
, while MetLife expects a tax hit of $13 million.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The tax, which went into effect Jan. 1, is set=
 to cost S&P 500 companies $1.6 billion in the second quarter, accordin=
g to preliminary data from S&P Dow Jones Indices, a unit of ratings fir=
m S&P Global. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Despite the higher tax obligations, companies =
are largely shrugging off the tax. From April through June, S&P 500 com=
panies are expected to spend around $169 billion on stock buybacks, down ab=
out 20% from both the first quarter of this year and from a year ago, the p=
reliminary S&P data show.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The math there is $169 billion of buybacks, 1%=
 tax, $1.6 billion of taxes.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1=
4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom=
-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !impor=
tant; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[14]</span>
</a>=C2=A0If companies did $169 billion of dividends instead, there=E2=80=
=99d be =E2=80=A6=C2=A0not $33.8 billion of taxes to pay (20% of $169 billi=
on), because some shareholders are not taxable or pay lower rates than 20%,=
 but a lot more than $1.6 billion anyway.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=
=3D"#footnote-15" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important=
; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-sty=
le: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inl=
ine-block;">
<span>[15]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Buybacks are more expensive than they used to be, but still a tax=
 bargain compared to dividends.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">By the way, this is a thing I did not know abo=
ut the buyback tax:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The 1% tax=E2=80=94which was a las=
t-minute addition to the climate, health and tax law known as the Inflation=
 Reduction Act passed last year=E2=80=94is levied on net buybacks, meaning =
total shares repurchased minus new shares issued during the year.</p></bloc=
kquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly91c2NvZGUuaG91c2UuZ292L3ZpZXcu=
eGh0bWw_cmVxPSh0aXRsZToyNiUyMHNlY3Rpb246NDUwMSUyMGVkaXRpb246cHJlbGltKQ/607f=
07ceef4b8524a319a23cB56ac8d9d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscop=
e" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> the statutory text</a>; section 450=
1(b)(3) says that the taxable amount of buybacks =E2=80=9Cshall be reduced =
by the fair market value of any stock issued by the covered corporation dur=
ing the taxable year, including the fair market value of any stock issued o=
r provided to employees.=E2=80=9D One <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly9oYnIub3JnLzIwMTQvMDkvcHJvZml=
0cy13aXRob3V0LXByb3NwZXJpdHk/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8ad76fb2" itemprop=3D=
"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> co=
mplaint</a> that people sometimes <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbus=
iness.com/click/32488619.288425/aHR0cHM6Ly9jb3JwZ292Lmxhdy5oYXJ2YXJkLmVkdS8=
yMDE5LzEwLzMwL2RpbHV0aW9uLWRpc2Nsb3N1cmUtZXF1aXR5LWNvbXBlbnNhdGlvbi1hbmQtYn=
V5YmFja3Mv/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4a700d42" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemsc=
ope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoratio=
n: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000;=
 border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> have</a> about stock=
 buybacks is that they are a way to sort of disguise executive compensation=
: A company=C2=A0pays its executives $100 million worth of stock, then it b=
uys back $100 million worth of stock to avoid the dilutive impact of those =
payments. I have never found this complaint that compelling (it=E2=80=99s n=
ot=C2=A0<em>disguised=C2=A0</em>executive compensation; this is all disclos=
ed), but some people do, and if you do I guess you should be dissatisfied w=
ith the buyback tax. It specifically exempts buybacks that are used to offs=
et stock-based compensation.</p>
